text:
Git branching is a powerful way to organize work in version control. Instead of everyone editing the same files directly, you create separate feature branches for new tasks. For example, if you're adding a login feature, you work on a branch named "feature-login" while the main branch stays stable.

Once your feature is complete, you can merge it back into the main branch. In many workflows, you first open a pull request to review the changes with teammates. They can comment on the code, suggest improvements, or approve it. After approval, the merge happens. This keeps the main branch clean and reliable, forming an organized version control workflow for teams.
